seoTitle: AutoChips Technical Support | Application Notes & Design Guides - LiTong
seoDescription: Access AutoChips technical documentation, application notes, and design guides. LiTong distributor provides expert FAE support and selection guidance for automotive IC designs.

faqs.question: What technical documentation does LiTong provide for AutoChips products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical documentation for AutoChips products including detailed datasheets with electrical specifications and timing diagrams, application notes covering common automotive design scenarios, reference designs with complete schematics and PCB layouts, and AUTOSAR MCAL drivers for software development. All documentation is available in English and Chinese and can be downloaded from our website or requested directly from our FAE team. We also provide customized application notes for specific customer requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our technical library or contact LiTong FAEs for specific documentation requests.

faqs.question: How can I request samples of AutoChips products?
faqs.answer: LiTong offers free samples for qualified automotive design projects. To request samples, contact our sales team with your company information, project details, and required part numbers. Samples typically ship within 2-3 business days for standard products. For automotive qualification requirements, we can provide AEC-Q100 compliance certificates and PPAP documentation. We also provide evaluation kits that include multiple related products for comprehensive system evaluation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong sales with your automotive project details to request samples for evaluation.

faqs.question: What design support services does LiTong offer?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive design support services including product selection guidance based on automotive application requirements, schematic review and optimization suggestions, PCB layout recommendations for EMC compliance, AUTOSAR software integration support, and functional safety consulting for ASIL applications. Our FAE team has extensive experience with AutoChips products across passenger vehicles, commercial vehicles, and new energy vehicle applications. We also offer on-site support for complex projects and can provide customized training for customer engineering teams.
faqs.decisionGuide: Engage LiTong FAEs early in your automotive design cycle for optimal product selection and application guidance.

faqs.question: How do I troubleshoot issues with AutoChips products in my design?
faqs.answer: For troubleshooting assistance, first review the datasheet and application notes for common issues and recommended configurations. Check power supply sequencing, decoupling capacitor placement, and signal integrity. Verify AEC-Q100 qualification requirements are met for automotive applications. If issues persist, contact LiTong technical support with detailed information including schematic snippets, scope captures of problematic signals, and description of observed behavior versus expected behavior. Our FAE team can provide remote debugging support or schedule on-site visits for complex automotive issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Document your issue thoroughly before contacting support. Include schematics, waveforms, and detailed problem description.

faqs.question: Are AUTOSAR MCAL drivers available for AutoChips MCUs?
faqs.answer: AUTOSAR MCAL (Microcontroller Driver) software is available for AutoChips automotive MCUs. The MCAL drivers provide standardized software interfaces for microcontroller peripherals including GPIO, ADC, PWM, CAN, LIN, and SPI. The drivers are compliant with AUTOSAR 4.x standards and support various compiler toolchains. AutoChips provides MCAL drivers, configuration tools, and integration guides. LiTong can provide MCAL software packages and technical support for AUTOSAR integration.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong to request AUTOSAR MCAL packages and integration support.

faqs.question: What is the lead time for AutoChips products?
faqs.answer: Standard lead time for AutoChips products is 6-8 weeks for moderate volumes. LiTong maintains safety stock of popular automotive products for immediate delivery. For large automotive production orders, lead times can be negotiated with factory support. We recommend customers provide rolling forecasts to ensure supply continuity. For urgent automotive production requirements, please contact our sales team to check current inventory status and expedite options. Long-term supply agreements are available for high-volume automotive customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Plan procurement with standard 6-8 week lead times. Contact sales for current stock and expedited delivery.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide training on AutoChips products?
faqs.answer: LiTong offers product training and application workshops for customer engineering teams. Training topics include AutoChips product portfolio overview, automotive MCU selection guides, motor control design best practices, functional safety implementation, and AUTOSAR software development. Training can be conducted at customer facilities, LiTong offices, or via web conference. Customized training programs can be developed for specific customer needs including hands-on workshops with evaluation boards.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong marketing to schedule product training for your automotive engineering team.

faqs.question: How can I get cross-reference information for AutoChips products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides cross-reference information to help customers find AutoChips alternatives to international automotive semiconductor products. Our FAE team can generate detailed comparison matrices showing electrical specifications, package options, AEC-Q100 qualification, and pricing for equivalent products. Cross-references are available for automotive MCUs, power management ICs, motor drivers, and sensor interfaces. Contact our technical support with the competitor part numbers you need to cross-reference, and we will provide recommended AutoChips alternatives with detailed comparison data.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide competitor part numbers to LiTong FAEs for cross-reference recommendations.

articles.title: Automotive MCU Selection Guide

author.name: Dr. Michael Chen
author.title: Principal FAE - Automotive Electronics
author.bio: 15+ years experience in automotive electronics design and applications, specializing in automotive MCUs and functional safety

articles.summary: This comprehensive guide helps engineers select the right automotive MCU for vehicle applications. Covers key parameters including processing performance, memory, safety levels, and automotive qualification with practical selection criteria.
articles.content:
Automotive microcontrollers are the foundation of modern vehicle electronic systems. Selecting the right automotive MCU requires understanding your application requirements and matching them to device specifications while ensuring AEC-Q100 qualification. This guide provides a systematic approach to automotive MCU selection.

**Understanding Key Parameters**

Processing performance depends on core architecture and clock frequency. AutoChips offers Cortex-M0+ at 48MHz for simple control tasks, Cortex-M3 at 72MHz for general applications, and Cortex-M4F at 120MHz for high-performance motor control and signal processing. Select performance level based on algorithm complexity and response time requirements.

Memory requirements include Flash for program storage and RAM for data and stack. Typical automotive applications require 64KB to 512KB Flash depending on software complexity. Allow 20-30% margin for future firmware updates. ECC memory protection is essential for safety-critical applications.

Functional safety level (ASIL) determines required hardware safety features. ASIL-B applications require ECC memory and clock monitoring. ASIL-D applications require dual-core lockstep, comprehensive memory protection, and extensive diagnostic capabilities.

**Selection Process**

1. Define your automotive requirements: operating temperature, safety level, communication needs, and EMC requirements.
2. Identify critical parameters based on your application type and safety requirements.
3. Use parametric search to find AEC-Q100 qualified candidates meeting your requirements.
4. Evaluate trade-offs between performance, safety, power, and cost.
5. Request samples and evaluate in your actual automotive environment.

**Application-Specific Recommendations**

For body control modules: Use AC7801x series with CAN-FD and LIN interfaces. For motor control: Select AC7840x with advanced motor control peripherals and FPU. For safety-critical systems: Choose ASIL-D capable MCUs with hardware safety features.

As an authorized AutoChips distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive automotive MCU selection support including parametric searches, sample evaluation, and application guidance. Contact our FAE team for personalized recommendations.

faeInsights.title: Principal FAE - Automotive Electronics
faeInsights.content: After helping hundreds of automotive customers select MCUs, I've learned that the most common mistake is underestimating safety requirements. Engineers often select MCUs based on performance alone while ignoring ASIL requirements that emerge later in development. My recommendation is to determine safety requirements early - even if your current application is non-safety-critical, future derivatives may require ASIL compliance. For automotive applications, I always recommend starting with AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualified devices to ensure temperature range compatibility. The AC7801x series offers excellent value for body control applications with good performance and competitive pricing. For safety-critical applications, the AC7840x series provides comprehensive ASIL-D features at a fraction of the cost of international competitors. One insight from the field: automotive EMC requirements are becoming increasingly stringent. Select MCUs with good EMC characteristics and plan PCB layout carefully from the start. Retrofitting EMC fixes is expensive and time-consuming.
practicalTips: Determine ASIL requirements early in the design cycle
Use AEC-Q100 Grade 1 devices for all automotive applications
Plan for 20-30% Flash memory margin for future updates
Consider EMC characteristics in MCU selection
Evaluate supply chain security for automotive production

decisionTree.action: Select AC7840x series with dual-core lockstep
Select AC7840x with Cortex-M4F
Select AC7801x for best value
Select AC7840x at 120MHz

keyTakeaways: Understand key selection criteria for optimal product choice
Consider both technical specifications and application requirements
Leverage reference designs to accelerate development
Consult FAE team for complex application challenges

customerCases.application: Body control module for passenger vehicle
customerCases.challenge: Required cost-effective MCU with CAN-FD and LIN interfaces for door control module. Previous MCU had supply constraints and high cost.
customerCases.solution: Implemented AC78013 MCU with integrated communication interfaces. Optimized PCB layout for automotive EMC.
customerCases.feedback: Achieved 30% cost reduction with equivalent performance. Supply secured for production ramp.

faqs.question: What is AEC-Q100 qualification and why is it important?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q100 is the Automotive Electronics Council qualification standard for integrated circuits. It defines stress test qualifications including high-temperature operating life, temperature cycling, and ESD testing. Grade 1 qualification (-40°C to +125°C) is required for most automotive applications. Using AEC-Q100 qualified components ensures reliability in harsh automotive environments and meets OEM requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always select AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualified MCUs for automotive applications.

faqs.question: How do I determine required ASIL level for my application?
faqs.answer: ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) is determined by hazard analysis and risk assessment following ISO 26262. Factors include severity of potential harm, exposure probability, and controllability. Body control typically requires QM or ASIL-A. Safety-related systems like braking require ASIL-D. LiTong can provide guidance on ASIL determination and safety architecture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong for ASIL assessment and safety concept development support.

faqs.question: What memory size do I need for my automotive application?
faqs.answer: Automotive MCU memory requirements depend on software complexity. Simple control applications need 64-128KB Flash. Complex applications with communication stacks need 256-512KB Flash. Always include 20-30% margin for future updates. RAM requirements depend on stack usage and data buffers. Typical applications need 16-128KB RAM.
faqs.decisionGuide: Estimate software size and add 30% margin. Contact LiTong for memory sizing assistance.

faqs.question: What communication interfaces do I need?
faqs.answer: Modern automotive MCUs need CAN-FD for high-speed vehicle communication, LIN for low-cost body electronics, and SPI/I2C for peripheral communication. Ethernet is increasingly required for ADAS applications. FlexRay may be needed for high-speed deterministic communication. Select MCUs with sufficient communication instances for your architecture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Include CAN-FD and LIN for most applications. Add Ethernet for ADAS connectivity.

faqs.question: How do I evaluate EMC performance of automotive MCUs?
faqs.answer: Automotive MCU EMC evaluation includes radiated emissions, conducted emissions, and immunity testing. Look for MCUs with slew rate control, spread spectrum clocking, and good power supply rejection. Request EMC test reports from the manufacturer. Plan PCB layout with proper grounding and filtering from the start.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request EMC test data and plan PCB layout carefully for automotive EMC compliance.

articles.title: Automotive Power Management IC Selection Guide

author.name: Jennifer Liu
author.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Power
author.bio: 12+ years experience in automotive power management and battery systems

articles.summary: Learn how to select automotive power management ICs for vehicle applications. This guide covers voltage regulation, load dump protection, functional safety, and thermal management for automotive ECU power supply design.
articles.content:
Automotive power management ICs provide stable, reliable power for vehicle electronic control units in harsh electrical environments. Selecting the right PMIC requires understanding automotive electrical conditions, safety requirements, and thermal constraints.

**Automotive Electrical Environment**

Vehicle electrical systems present challenging conditions including cold crank (voltage dips to 3.5V), load dump (voltage spikes to 40V), and reverse battery connection. Automotive PMICs must withstand these conditions while maintaining regulated output voltage.

Input voltage range is critical for automotive applications. Standard 12V vehicle systems require PMICs with 3.5V to 40V input range. Heavy-duty vehicles may require 60V capability. The PMIC must maintain regulation across the entire input range.

**Key Parameters**

Load dump protection is essential for automotive PMICs. The device must withstand 40V transients per ISO 7637-2 without damage. Internal protection is preferred over external TVS diodes to reduce BOM cost.

Functional safety features are required for ASIL-compliant systems. Safety PMICs include voltage monitoring, watchdog timers, and independent safety outputs. Redundant regulators provide backup power for safety-critical functions.

Thermal management is critical in automotive applications with high ambient temperatures. Calculate power dissipation and ensure adequate heat sinking. Junction temperature should be kept below 125°C for long-term reliability.

**Selection Guidelines**

For general ECU power: Use AC7801-LDO with load dump protection. For safety-critical systems: Select AC7840-PMIC with monitoring and ASIL support. For high-current applications: Consider switching regulators for better efficiency.

As an authorized AutoChips distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive PMIC selection support and automotive power design guidance.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Power
faeInsights.content: Automotive power supply design is often underestimated in complexity. The most common issue I see is inadequate protection against load dump conditions - designers use standard industrial PMICs that fail in vehicle electrical environments. I always recommend using automotive-qualified PMICs with integrated load dump protection. Another critical point is thermal management in sealed ECU enclosures. Calculate worst-case power dissipation at maximum ambient temperature and provide adequate PCB copper or heatsinks. For safety-critical applications, redundant power supplies with independent monitoring are essential. Don't rely on a single regulator for ASIL-D systems. One often overlooked aspect is transient response - automotive loads can change rapidly, and slow transient response can cause MCU brownouts. Select PMICs with fast transient response or add sufficient output capacitance.
practicalTips: Always use automotive-qualified PMICs with load dump protection
Calculate thermal design for worst-case conditions in sealed enclosures
Implement redundant supplies for safety-critical applications
Verify transient response under rapid load changes
Add sufficient input and output filtering for EMC compliance

decisionTree.action: Select AC7840-PMIC with safety monitoring
Consider switching regulator for efficiency
Select AC7801-LDO with load dump protection
Select low-Iq LDO for standby power

keyTakeaways: Understand key selection criteria for optimal product choice
Consider both technical specifications and application requirements
Leverage reference designs to accelerate development
Consult FAE team for complex application challenges

customerCases.challenge: Required PMIC with load dump protection and low quiescent current for always-on circuits. Previous solution required external protection components.
customerCases.solution: Implemented AC7801-LDO with integrated 40V load dump protection. Optimized thermal design for 85°C ambient operation.
customerCases.feedback: Eliminated external TVS diodes reducing BOM cost 15%. Reliable operation through all automotive electrical tests.

faqs.question: What is load dump and why is protection important?
faqs.answer: Load dump is a voltage transient that occurs when the battery is disconnected while the alternator is charging. Voltage can spike to 40V for hundreds of milliseconds. Without protection, this transient can damage ECU electronics. Automotive PMICs include load dump protection to withstand these transients.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always select PMICs with integrated load dump protection to 40V per ISO 7637-2.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in automotive PMICs?
faqs.answer: Power dissipation in linear regulators is P = (Vin - Vout) × Iout. For example, with 14V input, 3.3V output, and 100mA load, dissipation is 1.07W. Calculate for worst-case conditions including maximum input voltage and load current. Ensure thermal design can handle worst-case dissipation at maximum ambient temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate worst-case power dissipation and design thermal management accordingly.

faqs.question: What safety features do automotive PMICs include?
faqs.answer: Automotive safety PMICs include voltage monitoring with programmable thresholds, watchdog timers for MCU monitoring, independent safety outputs for fault indication, and redundant regulators for backup power. These features support ASIL-compliant system design by providing independent monitoring of power supply integrity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select safety PMICs with monitoring features for ASIL applications.

faqs.question: When should I use switching regulators instead of LDOs?
faqs.answer: Use switching regulators when voltage differential is large (Vin >> Vout) or current is high (>500mA). Switching regulators are more efficient but generate more EMI and require external inductors. LDOs are simpler, cheaper, and quieter but dissipate more power when input-output voltage difference is large.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use LDOs for low current and small voltage differential. Use switching regulators for high current or large voltage differential.

faqs.question: What input filtering is required for automotive PMICs?
faqs.answer: Automotive PMICs require input filtering to suppress conducted emissions and protect against transients. Use ceramic capacitors (10-47uF) close to the input pin for high-frequency filtering. Add TVS diodes for additional protection if required. Follow automotive EMC design guidelines for filtering component selection and placement.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement proper input filtering following automotive EMC guidelines.

articles.title: Automotive Motor Control Design Guide

author.name: Dr. Michael Chen
author.title: Principal FAE - Motor Control
author.bio: 15+ years experience in automotive motor control and power electronics

articles.summary: Complete guide to designing automotive motor control systems using AutoChips motor drivers and MCUs. Covers motor types, control algorithms, thermal management, and safety considerations for vehicle applications.
articles.content:
Automotive motor control systems drive fans, pumps, and actuators throughout the vehicle. This guide covers the fundamentals of automotive motor control design using AutoChips motor drivers and MCUs.

**Motor Types in Automotive Applications**

Brushless DC (BLDC) motors are widely used for fans and pumps due to high efficiency and reliability. BLDC motors require electronic commutation which can be sensorless (using BEMF detection) or sensored (using Hall sensors).

3-phase AC induction motors and permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSM) are used for high-power applications like electric power steering and traction drives. These motors require sophisticated control algorithms like Field Oriented Control (FOC).

Stepper motors provide precise position control for HVAC flap actuators and headlight leveling systems.

**Control Algorithms**

Trapezoidal commutation is the simplest BLDC control method, providing six-step commutation based on rotor position. It offers good efficiency but produces torque ripple.

Sinusoidal commutation provides smoother operation by driving motor phases with sine wave currents. It reduces torque ripple and audible noise compared to trapezoidal commutation.

Field Oriented Control (FOC) is the most advanced method, providing precise torque and flux control for high-performance applications. FOC enables efficient operation across the entire speed range.

**Design Considerations**

Thermal management is critical for motor drivers. Calculate power dissipation including conduction losses and switching losses. Provide adequate PCB copper or heatsinks for heat dissipation.

EMC considerations include switching noise from PWM operation. Use proper filtering and shielding to meet automotive EMC requirements.

Safety features are required for ASIL-compliant motor control. Include redundant current sensing, hardware over-current protection, and diagnostic capabilities.

As an authorized AutoChips distributor, LiTong provides motor control design support including algorithm tuning and thermal management guidance.

faeInsights.title: Principal FAE - Motor Control
faeInsights.content: Motor control design requires attention to both electrical and mechanical considerations. The most common issue I encounter is inadequate characterization of the motor itself - designers use datasheet values that may not match the actual motor parameters. I always recommend measuring motor resistance, inductance, and BEMF constant under operating conditions. Another critical aspect is current sensing accuracy. For FOC control, current measurement errors directly affect torque control precision. Use high-quality current sense resistors and proper PCB layout for Kelvin connections. Thermal management is often underestimated - motor drivers can dissipate significant power at high currents. Calculate worst-case thermal conditions including maximum ambient temperature and stalled motor conditions. For safety-critical applications like electric power steering, redundant current sensing and independent safety monitoring are essential. Don't rely solely on software protection for safety systems.
practicalTips: Characterize motor parameters under actual operating conditions
Use Kelvin connections for accurate current sensing
Design thermal management for worst-case conditions including stall
Implement hardware over-current protection for safety
Consider sensorless control limitations at low speeds

decisionTree.action: Implement FOC with AC7840-MOTOR
Use trapezoidal commutation with AC7801-MOTOR
Use sinusoidal commutation with higher PWM frequency
Implement redundant sensing and safety monitoring

keyTakeaways: Understand key selection criteria for optimal product choice
Consider both technical specifications and application requirements
Leverage reference designs to accelerate development
Consult FAE team for complex application challenges

customerCases.challenge: Required quiet, efficient blower control with variable speed. Previous solution had audible noise at certain speeds.
customerCases.solution: Implemented AC7801-MOTOR with sinusoidal commutation and 20kHz PWM. Optimized current loop tuning.
customerCases.feedback: Achieved quiet operation across all speeds with 92% efficiency.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sensored and sensorless motor control?
faqs.answer: Sensored control uses Hall sensors or encoders to detect rotor position for commutation. Sensorless control uses BEMF detection to determine rotor position. Sensored control works at all speeds including stall but adds cost and wiring. Sensorless control is lower cost but has limitations at very low speeds where BEMF is weak.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sensorless for fans and pumps. Use sensored for applications requiring torque control at low speeds.

faqs.question: How do I select PWM frequency for motor control?
faqs.answer: PWM frequency selection balances switching losses, current ripple, and audible noise. Higher frequencies (20-50kHz) reduce current ripple and audible noise but increase switching losses. Lower frequencies (5-15kHz) reduce switching losses but may cause audible noise and higher current ripple. For automotive applications, 20kHz is typical to avoid audible noise.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 20kHz for quiet operation. Use lower frequencies for higher efficiency in noise-tolerant applications.

faqs.question: What current sensing methods are used in motor control?
faqs.answer: Motor control uses low-side sensing, high-side sensing, or inline sensing. Low-side sensing is simple but cannot detect ground shorts. High-side sensing can detect all faults but requires high common-mode voltage capability. Inline sensing provides best performance for FOC but requires two sensors for 3-phase motors. Select based on safety requirements and cost constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use inline sensing for FOC applications. Use low-side sensing for cost-sensitive applications without ground short concerns.

faqs.question: How do I prevent motor over-temperature?
faqs.answer: Motor over-temperature protection includes current limiting to reduce heating, temperature monitoring using thermistors or RTDs, and thermal models to estimate motor temperature. Implement derating at high ambient temperatures. For safety-critical applications, redundant temperature monitoring is recommended.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement current limiting and temperature monitoring for motor protection.

faqs.question: What causes motor torque ripple and how do I minimize it?
faqs.answer: Torque ripple is caused by commutation timing errors, current measurement errors, and PWM switching. Minimize ripple by using sinusoidal commutation instead of trapezoidal, optimizing current loop bandwidth, using high-resolution current sensing, and increasing PWM frequency. Proper motor parameter identification is essential for FOC torque ripple minimization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sinusoidal commutation and optimize control loops to minimize torque ripple.

articles.title: Automotive EMC Design Best Practices

author.name: Jennifer Liu
author.title: Senior FAE - EMC Engineering
author.bio: 12+ years experience in automotive EMC design and testing

articles.summary: Essential EMC design guidelines for automotive electronics using AutoChips products. Covers grounding, filtering, shielding, and PCB layout for CISPR 25 and ISO 11452 compliance.
articles.content:
Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) is critical for automotive electronics to ensure reliable operation in the presence of electromagnetic disturbances and to prevent interference with other vehicle systems. This guide covers EMC design best practices for automotive ECUs using AutoChips products.

**Automotive EMC Requirements**

Automotive EMC standards include CISPR 25 for conducted and radiated emissions, ISO 11452 for immunity to radiated disturbances, and ISO 7637-2 for immunity to conducted disturbances on power lines. OEMs typically have additional requirements that may be more stringent than international standards.

Emissions requirements limit electromagnetic noise generated by the ECU that could interfere with vehicle radios and communication systems. Immunity requirements ensure the ECU operates correctly when exposed to electromagnetic disturbances from vehicle systems and external sources.

**PCB Design for EMC**

Ground plane design is fundamental to EMC performance. Use solid ground planes without splits to provide low-impedance return paths. Route high-speed signals over continuous ground planes to minimize loop area and radiation.

Power supply decoupling is essential for reducing conducted emissions. Place ceramic capacitors (0.1uF to 10uF) close to IC power pins. Use multiple vias to connect capacitor grounds to the ground plane.

Signal routing should minimize loop areas and avoid crossing split planes. Keep high-speed digital signals away from sensitive analog circuits. Use 45-degree angles or curved traces rather than right angles to reduce reflections.

**Filtering and Shielding**

Input filtering on power lines reduces conducted emissions and improves immunity. Use common mode chokes and ceramic capacitors for effective filtering. Filter all external connections including communication lines and sensor inputs.

Shielding may be required for high-frequency circuits or sensitive analog sections. Use grounded metal enclosures or shielding cans over critical circuits. Ensure proper grounding of shields to avoid antenna effects.

**Testing and Validation**

Pre-compliance testing during development helps identify EMC issues early. Use near-field probes to locate emission sources. Conduct immunity testing with appropriate coupling methods.

As an authorized AutoChips distributor, LiTong provides EMC design review services and testing support.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - EMC Engineering
faeInsights.content: EMC design is often treated as an afterthought, leading to expensive redesigns and testing failures. The key insight is that EMC must be designed in from the start - it cannot be fixed at the end. I always recommend starting with a solid ground plane design and proper power distribution. The most common EMC issue I see is inadequate decoupling - designers place capacitors too far from ICs or use insufficient via connections. My rule: if you can fit another component between the capacitor and the IC, the capacitor is too far away. Another critical aspect is cable routing - external cables act as antennas for both emissions and immunity. Keep cable lengths short, use proper shielding, and implement common mode filtering at cable entry points. For switching power supplies and motor drives, switching frequency and edge rates significantly affect EMC. Slower edges reduce high-frequency emissions but increase switching losses. Find the right balance for your application. Finally, don't underestimate the importance of proper grounding - a good ground plane is the foundation of EMC performance.
practicalTips: Design EMC from the start - cannot be fixed at the end
Place decoupling capacitors as close as possible to IC power pins
Use solid ground planes without splits for low impedance return paths
Minimize loop areas in high-speed signal routing
Implement filtering at all external cable entry points

decisionTree.action: Implement shielding and aggressive filtering
Add common mode filtering at all cable entries
Use separate ground areas and shielding
Optimize PCB layout before adding filtering components

keyTakeaways: Understand key selection criteria for optimal product choice
Consider both technical specifications and application requirements
Leverage reference designs to accelerate development
Consult FAE team for complex application challenges

customerCases.application: Body control module with multiple communication interfaces
customerCases.challenge: ECU failed CISPR 25 radiated emissions at FM radio frequencies. Previous design required multiple redesign cycles.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned PCB with continuous ground plane, optimized decoupling placement, and added common mode filters on CAN and LIN lines.
customerCases.feedback: Passed CISPR 25 Class 5 with 6dB margin. No redesign cycles required for production.

faqs.question: What is CISPR 25 and why is it important?
faqs.answer: CISPR 25 is the international standard for radio disturbance characteristics of vehicles and devices intended for use in vehicles. It defines limits for conducted and radiated emissions to ensure vehicle electronics do not interfere with radio reception. Automotive OEMs require CISPR 25 compliance for all electronic modules. The standard has different classes with Class 5 being the most stringent for critical applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for CISPR 25 Class 5 to meet most OEM requirements with margin.

faqs.question: How do I design ground planes for automotive EMC?
faqs.answer: Use solid, continuous ground planes without splits or slots. Route high-speed signals over continuous ground to minimize loop area. Connect all ground areas at a single point to avoid ground loops. Use multiple vias for ground connections to reduce impedance. For mixed-signal designs, separate analog and digital ground areas with single point connection near the ADC.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use solid ground planes and minimize slots for best EMC performance.

faqs.question: What filtering is required for automotive ECUs?
faqs.answer: Automotive ECUs require filtering on all external connections. Power lines need common mode chokes and ceramic capacitors for conducted emissions. Communication lines (CAN, LIN) need common mode filters for noise suppression. Sensor inputs need RC filtering to suppress high-frequency noise. Filter components should be placed at the PCB edge near connector entry points.
faqs.decisionGuide: Filter all external connections including power, communication, and sensor lines.

faqs.question: How do I reduce radiated emissions from switching circuits?
faqs.answer: Reduce radiated emissions by minimizing loop areas in switching circuits, using shielded inductors, implementing snubber circuits, and optimizing switching edge rates. Place input and output capacitors close to switching devices to minimize high-frequency current loops. Use multi-layer PCBs with ground planes to provide shielding. Consider spread spectrum clocking if applicable.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize switching loop areas and use proper filtering to reduce radiated emissions.

faqs.question: What immunity testing is required for automotive ECUs?
faqs.answer: Automotive ECUs must pass immunity testing per ISO 11452 (radiated immunity) and ISO 7637-2 (conducted immunity). Radiated immunity tests exposure to electromagnetic fields from 1MHz to 3GHz. Conducted immunity tests injection of disturbances on power and signal lines. OEMs may have additional requirements including bulk current injection and transient immunity tests.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for ISO 11452 and ISO 7637-2 immunity requirements.

articles.title: AUTOSAR Integration Guide for AutoChips MCUs

author.name: Jennifer Liu
author.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Software
author.bio: 10+ years experience in automotive software development and AUTOSAR integration

articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for integrating AutoChips MCUs into AUTOSAR software architecture. Covers MCAL configuration, ECU abstraction layer, and application software development.
articles.content:
AUTOSAR (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture) is the standardized software architecture widely adopted in the automotive industry. This guide covers the integration of AutoChips MCUs into AUTOSAR-based systems, providing practical guidance for software developers and system architects.

**AUTOSAR Architecture Overview**

AUTOSAR defines a layered software architecture consisting of the Application Layer (ASW), Runtime Environment (RTE), and Basic Software (BSW). The BSW is further divided into ECU Abstraction Layer, Service Layer, and Microcontroller Driver Layer (MCAL). AutoChips provides MCAL drivers that interface between the microcontroller hardware and the upper AUTOSAR layers.

**MCAL Configuration**

The Microcontroller Driver Layer (MCAL) provides low-level drivers for microcontroller peripherals including GPIO, ADC, PWM, CAN, LIN, and SPI. AutoChips MCAL drivers are compliant with AUTOSAR 4.x standards and support various compiler toolchains including Tasking, HighTec, and Green Hills.

MCAL configuration is performed using EB tresos Studio or similar configuration tools. The configuration process involves: selecting enabled peripherals, configuring clock settings and prescalers, setting up pin multiplexing and alternate functions, and configuring interrupt priorities and routing.

**Integration Best Practices**

Successful AUTOSAR integration requires attention to timing, memory, and communication aspects. Ensure MCAL initialization completes before BSW initialization. Configure memory sections according to linker scripts provided by AutoChips. Implement proper error handling and diagnostic event management.

As an authorized AutoChips distributor, LiTong provides AUTOSAR MCAL packages, configuration support, and integration consulting services.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Software
faeInsights.content: AUTOSAR integration is often more complex than anticipated, especially for teams new to the standard. The most common issue I encounter is improper MCAL configuration leading to peripheral timing problems. I always recommend starting with a reference configuration from AutoChips and modifying it incrementally rather than creating from scratch. Another critical aspect is memory configuration - AUTOSAR requires specific memory sections for code, data, and calibration parameters. Ensure your linker script matches the MCAL requirements. For debugging, use the DET (Default Error Tracer) to catch configuration errors early. The RTE generation process can be confusing initially - understand the contract between SWC ports before implementing. Finally, don't underestimate the learning curve for AUTOSAR. Plan for training and expect the first project to take longer than traditional embedded development.
practicalTips: Start with reference MCAL configuration and modify incrementally
Ensure linker scripts match AUTOSAR memory section requirements
Use DET for early detection of configuration errors
Understand RTE contracts before implementing SWC interfaces
Plan for AUTOSAR learning curve in project schedules

decisionTree.action: Plan for training and extended development time
Consider classic AUTOSAR vs adaptive based on requirements
Invest in proper tooling and configuration management
Engage with OEM early to understand specific requirements

keyTakeaways: Understand key selection criteria for optimal product choice
Consider both technical specifications and application requirements
Leverage reference designs to accelerate development
Consult FAE team for complex application challenges

customerCases.application: Body control module with AUTOSAR software
customerCases.challenge: Team needed to migrate from proprietary software to AUTOSAR for OEM compliance. Limited experience with AUTOSAR configuration and integration.
customerCases.solution: Used AutoChips MCAL with EB tresos configuration tool. Started with reference configuration and adapted for specific requirements. Implemented incremental testing approach.
customerCases.feedback: Successfully achieved AUTOSAR compliance within project timeline. Reference configuration accelerated development significantly.

quantitativeResults.efficiencyImprovement: Reference config saved 2 months development time

faqs.question: What AUTOSAR version does AutoChips MCAL support?
faqs.answer: AutoChips MCAL drivers support AUTOSAR Classic Platform version 4.2.2 and 4.4.0. The drivers are tested and validated against these AUTOSAR specifications. For Adaptive AUTOSAR applications, standard POSIX drivers can be used with AutoChips MCUs. Contact LiTong for specific AUTOSAR version requirements and compatibility information.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify AUTOSAR version requirements with your OEM before selecting MCAL version.

faqs.question: What configuration tools are supported?
faqs.answer: AutoChips MCAL supports industry-standard configuration tools including EB tresos Studio, Vector DaVinci Configurator, and ETAS ISOLAR. The MCAL delivery includes configuration files (ARXML) that can be imported into these tools. EB tresos is the most commonly used tool and has the most mature support for AutoChips MCAL.
faqs.decisionGuide: EB tresos Studio recommended for AutoChips MCAL configuration.

faqs.question: How do I debug AUTOSAR integration issues?
faqs.answer: AUTOSAR debugging requires understanding the layered architecture. Use DET (Default Error Tracer) to catch BSW and MCAL errors. Enable development error detection in MCAL configuration. Use RTE trace to monitor inter-SWC communication. For timing issues, use OS timing protection and analyze task scheduling. LiTong FAEs can provide debugging support for complex integration issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Enable DET and development error detection during integration phase.

faqs.question: What is the RTE and how does it work?
faqs.answer: RTE (Runtime Environment) is the middleware layer in AUTOSAR that enables communication between software components (SWCs). RTE provides standardized interfaces for inter-component communication. It handles data consistency and timing. RTE is generated based on component configuration.
faqs.decisionGuide: Understand RTE contracts before implementing SWC interfaces.

faqs.question: How do I configure MCAL for my application?
faqs.answer: MCAL configuration is done using EB tresos Studio or similar tools. Import the MCAL package, create a new project, and configure each peripheral using the graphical interface. Generate the configuration code and integrate it with your application. Validate the configuration using DET.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with reference configuration and modify for your application.
